description:
You are the configuration manager of a small project.  
    Three configuration files already exist in the directory “/home/user/configs”:

    1. /home/user/configs/app.conf  
    2. /home/user/configs/db.conf  
    3. /home/user/configs/web.conf  

    Your job is to create – and immediately verify – an integrity manifest so that
    future changes to any of these files can be detected automatically.

    What you must achieve
    ---------------------
    A. Generate a SHA-256 manifest called  
       /home/user/configs/manifest.sha256 – **exactly** one line per file, sorted
       alphabetically by file name (app.conf, db.conf, web.conf).  
       • Format of every line (no leading/trailing spaces):  
         “<64-char-hex-digest><two spaces><relative file name>”  
       • There must be one terminating LF (newline, “\n”) at the end of *each*
         line, including the very last one.

    B. Immediately verify the manifest you just produced and write an explicit
       result file called  
       /home/user/configs/verification.log with **exactly** the four lines shown
       below, in this order, each terminated by a single LF:

       1. OK: app.conf  
       2. OK: db.conf  
       3. OK: web.conf  
       4. SUMMARY: 3/3 files verified successfully  

       (There must be no extra blank lines before, after, or between the lines.)

    C. Do *not* modify the existing configuration files in any way.

    You will have to:
       • calculate SHA-256 digests for the three files,  
       • create the manifest file with the required format,  
       • compare every file against the manifest, and  
       • write the verification log exactly as specified.

    Complexity guideline
    --------------------
    The complete procedure normally takes about 5-10 shell commands
    (e.g. mkdir, sha256sum, cut/awk/sort, tee, test, echo, etc.).  
    Use any command-line tools available in a typical Linux environment
    – you do *not* have root privileges.

    When you are done, the directory /home/user/configs must contain *at least*
    the five files listed above and both new files must match the required
    formats byte-for-byte so that an automated test can simply read and compare
    them.
truth:
Directory & files that are present **before** the agent begins:

    /home/user/configs/            (existing directory, mode 755)
    ├── app.conf   – ASCII, 3 bytes, content exactly: “abc”   (no newline)
    ├── db.conf    – 0 bytes, *empty file*
    └── web.conf   – ASCII, 43 bytes, content exactly:
                     “The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog” (no newline)

    Correct SHA-256 digests for those exact byte sequences:
      app.conf : ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad
      db.conf  : e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855
      web.conf : d7a8fbb307d7809469ca9abcb0082e4f8d5651e46d3cdb762d02d0bf37c9e592

    Therefore the *expected* manifest file, byte-for-byte, is (each ⏎ represents
    a literal LF 0x0A):

        ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad␠␠app.conf⏎
        e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855␠␠db.conf⏎
        d7a8fbb307d7809469ca9abcb0082e4f8d5651e46d3cdb762d02d0bf37c9e592␠␠web.conf⏎

    (␠ represents a single space; there are *two* consecutive spaces between the
    checksum and the file name on every line.)

    The *expected* verification.log (again, ⏎ = LF) is:

        OK: app.conf⏎
        OK: db.conf⏎
        OK: web.conf⏎
        SUMMARY: 3/3 files verified successfully⏎

    Automated tests will:

    1. Confirm that /home/user/configs/manifest.sha256 exists and is an exact
       byte-for-byte match of the expected content above.
    2. Confirm that /home/user/configs/verification.log exists and is an exact
       byte-for-byte match of the expected content above.
    3. Confirm that the three original config files are unchanged (size &
       SHA-256 digests identical to the values listed above).
